speaker: interviewee
text: I've got one very important episode. See, my father needed some money in 1939. So he took my mother's jewelry, and he put it in a [? lombard. ?] And the [? lombard ?] was in Lodz. And they gave him a-- a ticket. And whoever showed the ticket could get out the money-- the-- the jewelry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with my father. I accompanied him on a horse and buggy to a little city where it was called [? Srotzk. ?] And this was very close to the-- to the Polish-- new German-Polish border. And when we came there, my father said he will go over the border with the certificate, and I have to wait till the smuggler who takes him over the border-- because there were a whole group of Jews who were growing. Then I can go back home and tell mother that everything went fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stood there in this little [? Srotzk, ?] I remember, till 3 o'clock. And then instead of the smugglers come back, my father came back. And he ran through the border. Because what happened is that they were all caught by the German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the German soldiers. And they were sent back to go back to [? Srotzk. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father said like this, what know what, [NON-ENGLISH]? I saw how you can go very easy without anything over the border, because the Poles were going there-- the old babushkas-- to the church. You will just go as a Pole to that-- through that border. And I said, yes, I will. And I got the certificate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you stop for a second?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I got the certificate. They put it in-- in a rubber condom. And they rolled it so tight with strings under. And I had to put it in my rectum. This how I was carrying the certificate. And when I would come to Lodz, I will get the certificate. Now, I went. I did that-- what my father said. And I-- I think it was already Sunday morning, or something like this. And I went direct through the highway-- I didn't go through the woods. And I saw shortly-- I don't remember the city where it was-- an old Polish woman. It was very slippery, and she was maybe 60 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her, [NON-ENGLISH], you can hold on my arm. I will help you. And we went through the-- like nothing-- through the-- you know, when they have those-- I don't remember what it called. They opened the thing, and-- and I think that the German-- the woman was going to church. So I thought-- yeah, Tuszyn. Now I remember the city. So we had to go to Tuszyn. But the church was between [? Srotzk ?] and Tuszyn on the other side. So she said she's going to church. And I told her I will help you go. And then, when I was on the other side, I went to the railroad station in Tuszyn, and I went to Lodz. So when I came to Lodz, to the place that-- there were supposed to be money, because-- German marks. Because I had to pay for whatever we were owning. The Jewish-- our neighbor who was supposed to leave their money, didn't leave any money. So there I was with my paper, illegal in-- in Tuszyn, which was already on the German the [NON-ENGLISH] they were called it. So what happened is I went to-- to Lodz-- from Tuszyn to Lodz, and in Lodz I had neighbors that they moved to Lodz many years ago. And they were very good friends of my mother. I went to them, and I said like this, listen, I need to get out of some this thing from the [? lombard. ?] And Israel [? Levich ?] says he's going to leave here money. He didn't leave any money. Can you do something about it? So they said that they want to lend me the-- the marks. And they had a Polish man-- he's a volksdeutsche-- who used to smuggle for them between Lodz and Warsaw. They were doing-- knitting-- knitting sweaters and little boots. So I said, OK. Get me in touch with this man.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man came, and we decided on a price, whatever I'm going to [? call ?] to pay him. I gave him the certificate. And the-- the-- I don't remember what their name was-- those-- those people that they gave me the money. And I waited. He came back the next time-- next day. He said, well, I was just staying in front, and they closed the door. They said to come tomorrow. My nose told me that it is not right. So there I am like a Pollack in there. I knew the address of the-- of the [? lombard. ?] And I go up there very early to be close to the window. I can see if this-- if he comes. And you know, I stayed at the window. I didn't see this man. And then they said that no more. They were not giving out any more-- those things that were hawked there. I go back to-- to the friends. And this goy comes. So he said, you know, they told me I have to go to the Gestapo there in Lodz, because I am getting-- it is not my-- my thing. He was just giving me a-- a bad story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him like this. Listen-- I knew I had nothing to lose. Listen, I said, you're lying, because I was at the [? lombard ?] at 8 o'clock in the morning. And I stood next to the window. And I saw everybody till they closed. And you didn't come. And you didn't know that-- that they don't give that stuff anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had to go to-- so I said, listen, I will tell you only one thing. You not going to go to the Gestapo. I am going to go to the Gestapo. I just threatened him. And I-- if you don't bring me that back, I am going to go to the Gestapo, and I will tell them that you took this paper, and that you just swindled me and the Germans out of this thing. And then I went. He said, no. And then he felt-- he told those other Jewish people, you probably think that I always-- because he lost-- he lost many times stuff from between Lodz and Warsaw. And I went followed him. They way he [? went-- ?] he lived in Lodz in the section that was called the Baluty. And I stood outside. He wasn't there. I went to his wife. And I said, listen, your husband took this, and they paid money, and now he's lying. He never came there. You talk to your husband, because otherwise I will go to the-- to the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she probably told him something, you know? And I got the jewelry back. And I put it in-- in a shoemaker was there. He put it in the-- took off the heel, and make a nest there, and put different things in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- once I was done with this business, I went to look up my Hashomer Hatzair friends. I went to one friend's house, and the father had a candy factory. He said his son is in Lithuania-- that the whole group went to Lithuania. But he said, there is one girl here. And her father was sick, so she didn't go. He gave me the address of this girl. I went to this girl house. And she said, look, she said, there is a meeting today, that a man came from Lithuania, and he's having this meeting. And he's organizing the Jewish underground in the big cities. And this was Mordechai Anielewicz. But at that time I didn't know who Mordechai Anielewicz was. And when I wanted to go to the meeting inside, I was not allowed to go to the meeting. You know, I still was from Hashomer Hatzair, from a different city. I had to wait. When he came back from the meeting, I told him, look, I am here on business for my parents. I have done everything. And I want to know what the movement says we should do with with our work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said like-- and I told him we meet every-- every week or every day in Meyer Sanyolevits's house. We have discussions. We do politics. We do [? think, ?] you know. And just to lift up the spirit of the other, younger-- younger members. So he told me, you know, I didn't-- Piotrkow was not on my list. But once you said that you already organized, that you having meetings, wait for me-- he had to do a few more things-- and I will go with you. I waited a few more days, and we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could not go back by train. We went through the Jewish cemetery. And the Polish cemetery was very close. The cemeteries are back-to-back. And I-- I jumped the fence. And he jumped the fence. And then we were already in a place where you can-- you cannot get to Piotrkow. You have to go a different way to the City of Belchatow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sat on one, you know-- I don't know-- a wagon. Quite a few people who were going Belchatow. So I said, listen, let's go to Belchatow, because my mother has a friend there. They moved to Belchatow. And we will go to her house. And from Belchatow we will go by foot the Piotrkow. It's 28 kilometers. So when we went there-- this I know it was Friday night when we came in. I didn't know that the rest of-- the [NON-ENGLISH] is what those people. But Mordechai Anielewicz and myself, we went along the wall. And I said, listen. When we'll see Shabbos candles, we'll just go in. Because they were afraid to let strangers in. So we went, and we saw a window with Shabbos candles, knocked at the door. And I told them, listen, I [INAUDIBLE] I have here friends, my mother's friends, the [INAUDIBLE], and I need-- I don't know where they are. First they didn't want to talk to me, you know? Then I told them, look. Those two sons were members of Hashomer Hatzair. And this man came from Russia. And he's going to give the parents a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, from their children. When she heard this-- and I told her about the [NON-ENGLISH], because it's they have one-- see, she saw that I know this family. So I went to that [NON-ENGLISH]. And they treated us beautifully. And the next day we had to go to Piotrkow. But the officers-- we needed a special permit to cross the border from, you know, into Piotrkow from Belchatow. So the office was opening up, I think, at 10 o'clock. So we decided like this. We going to go without a permit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, when we go there, I will talk to the-- to the Germans. And they will let us go. And if they will not let us go through, you will sit there and wait. I will go back. And we'll still be ahead of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there and we went to this-- I don't remember how it's called. To the place where those border patrols sit. He sees me, and he sees Mordechai Anielewicz. And he speaks to us German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I made a few jokes. And he asked if we smuggled something. I said, no. We don't smuggle anything. And I don't remember what I told him-- that want to go to the church or whatever, something like this. And he let us go. And-- and we walked from the Belchatow to Piotrkow. And it was 25 or 23 kilometers. And we came in. And I first put him-- I said, I'm going to bring Mordechai Anielewicz to Meyer's house, because all the meetings were meeting in Meyer's house. Then I will go home, and I will change. I brought him in, and they had a meeting. And Meyer saw through the window that I am coming with somebody. And Meyer was on a-- on a Hashomer Hatzair seminar with Mordechai Anielewicz. So I said, don't worry. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a hero even then? Mordechai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They send him there. The Jewish movement-- the Hashomer Hatzair-- send him back to Poland to organize the-- the Jewish youth. And-- well, and he went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, he was handsome. [LAUGHS] Secondly, I want to tell you that I had a crash on him. [LAUGHS] Had blue eyes, and he spoke impeccable Polish. He was small.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was very intelligent. I remember when we were walking, we were discussing [? Russell ?] or some other-- I don't remember. He was very knowledgeable. And he-- I think, as a young child, he lived in a Polish neighborhood, you know. And I liked him. What can I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I-- when I left him in Meyer's house, he was very intelligent, very nice spoken. He told me he has a girlfriend in-- in Warsaw and her name was-- I cannot remember. But I will remember her name. But we'll come back. And he said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no. I went home. I washed. I dressed. I came back. And he didn't even recognize me. [LAUGHS] And then they had meetings with Meyer, because Meyer and another man in our city, older, they were the leaders. And I was, like, the younger leader. I was not the first fiddle among those leaders. Only I did go, because I had a good face, you know-- a Polish face. And the funny thing is that one time-- because I'm going back somehow-- I went to look for my brother. And I couldn't-- because we didn't know what happened. He's not there. I went. And as I told you, I was wearing my coat like a cape. And my sleeve-- there was a Jewish armband in the packet. And I come into this place. And my brother sits with a Polish boy from the gymnasium. And they're talking, and they're joking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started to-- to talk with him. It was this Polish boy. He said, you know what? How about you and me going to the movies? I said, listen, I will go with a shegetz to the movie? My parents would kill me. I said, oh, I am so sorry, I told him. You know, I am Jewish, and I cannot go to the movies, because movies were forbidden for the Jews. So here right away he says, you know what? I have a passport for you. And I will, if you want, then you will buy that passport, and then we can go always to the movies. I said, OK. [SOB] Can you wait here? I will go ask my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: I run fast home. And I told my father, listen, there is a Polish gymnasist there where by-- they were turning wheat into a flour, wheat-- he says he has a Polish passport and he wants to sell it to me [SOBBING] for 50 zloty. And my father says, go and grab it, because it could save your life and our life because once you had a Polish passport, you could go outside the ghetto and buy food. You couldn't buy meat. But [? without ?] [? cards, ?] you could buy liver, you could buy heart, you know. You could buy lung. So I went fast back, and I made a-- a appointment with this Polish boy for the next day. I went on [NON-ENGLISH]. That's what the street was. Number 15. And he was waiting for me in that-- in that place. And this was outside the ghetto. So I had to-- I had to be without. And I didn't risk too far, because I know a way how to go. I went to there. I had my 50 zloties. I gave him the 50 zloties. He gave me the passport. I fast ran out looking if he didn't send, you know, a policeman after me. When I saw nobody, I just went back to the-- to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make the passport for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once I got the passport in my house, I looked at it. Gee, this was from an older woman. She was born in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think-- 13. And I was born in 1923. 10 years' difference. And we had to take off her picture and put my picture on. Meyer took a-- button the size of the-- of the stamp. We fastened my picture. And with a fine brush, he fixed the colors so it will be matching the red. And he closed in the seal. And then there another brush. He put in a few letters there, there were missing. And now the [INAUDIBLE], what we decided to do was to change from 13 to 18, because it's very easy to close in the other part of the three. And so I figured, well, it's still better. A woman can make herself looking older with lipstick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I had this [NON-ENGLISH] passport. It's changed to be 1918. And I have this trouble to learn how to falsify her signature. I can't do it. No matter what, I couldn't do it. So then I had a cousin who was doing some false papers, and I met him-- I don't want to go into it-- on the train. And he fixed for me a birthday certificate. He wrote it out, and he wrote my Christian parents, and the name of the parish. And he signed it. And now I had this paper [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Antonia ?] [? Kowalski ?] was my-- my mother's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was born [? Kowalski. ?] And that how I got this false certificate. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to the movies with that boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never went to the movies with him, because this wasn't my intention. I got the passport, and I never came there where they were doing the grinding of the flour. So now we could use this. And I went quite a few times out on that false passport. I went to the Polish butchers and I got-- without-- without ration cards-- liver, heart, lungs. And I will tell you only one thing. I brought the stuff home. My mother never, never ate a thing out of that food I brought. She fried the liver, put onions in it. She made the treif liver kosher with coals, you know. Never ate any of it. And then, I discussed this with my daddy. Listen, they have horse meat without cards in the Polish butcher. So my father said, you buy the horse meat, and we will not tell mother. And we will just come home and make, you know, hamburger out of the horse meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I buy this horse meat, I come back to the ghetto. And I tell, oh, I got-- you know, that I got this-- this ground beef. She makes everything the way it's supposed to be with garlic, with some onions. And on the end, she starts-- and she starts to fry it. And she said, you know, are you sure this is fresh meat? It's so dark. Horse meat is a much darker, you know. And I said, yeah. You see, she-- and we made her eat it. And-- and she didn't know that she was eating treif horse meat. You see? And-- and-- but the other, the liver and those things, she never wanted to eat. What became of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when the Germans surrounded our ghetto, when I run away with my brother, they were caught. They went into hiding-- my mother, my sister, and my father, and two other neighbors-- in the attic under the [? eaves. ?] Somebody hid in that place, was a deserter in World War I who was never caught. But when my parents went into it, one man was lived in our house, [? Shaiya ?] was his name, [? Shaiya ?] [? Zinger, ?] I think. And when he saw that my parents-- somehow they knew, because my father and the other member brought water to the-- to the hiding place so that they will have enough water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said like this to my father, Herschel, if you don't let me in with my family, nobody's going to go in. So what were they supposed to do? They let him in. And this was such a-- the place, it was only made for a few. They laid there like sardines. When one wanted to move, all of them had to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they stayed there, I think, three or six weeks, I think. One of those. When the ghetto was-- was clear of Jews, the Germans were going to get all those people from the hiding places. And they knew that there were hiding places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened is the attic had, like, dust. No-- not wood. You know what I mean? Yeah, sawdust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not sawdust. Just, like, [INAUDIBLE] whatever it was there. So when they went to get the-- the water [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from someplace-- I don't know if they went downstairs or-- they filled the water too much, and the water spilled, and makes spots in the attic. And when the German came out, they were shouting, are you in the house? And the water led them to the place where everybody was hiding. And this Mr. [? Shaiya ?] [? Zinger, ?] he was the first one to get out. He didn't want to wait anymore. He gave-- [INAUDIBLE] the German money. And I don't know if the German took the money, but everybody had to go out. They went down to the Jewish police station, which was in our [NON-ENGLISH]. And then they took them to the great synagogue. And all those Jews, then they send them to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never saw them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.
